Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 织锦寄夫 - Refers to the story of Jielan (织女), a legendary figure who wove a brocade for her husband. This allusion suggests the elder sister's self-doubt about her own skills.
  • 离魂奔婿 - Refers to the story of Qian Nv (倩女), a legendary figure who could leave her body to go to her husband. This allusion suggests the elder sister's inability to be with the man.
  • 鸾凤分飞，燕鸿交避 - These metaphors are commonly used in Chinese literature to describe the separation of lovers. '鸾凤' (phoenix and peacock) and '燕鸿' (swallow and wild goose) are symbols of love and fidelity.
  • 悠悠长恨，耿耿遐思 - These phrases are often used to describe deep and lasting feelings of sorrow and longing in Chinese poetry.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 再歌曰 - Formulaic phrase indicating the second song or verse in a sequence.
  • 三歌曰 - Formulaic phrase indicating the third song or verse.
  • 珠露零兮箫韵清 - Poetic phrase describing dewdrops falling and the clear sound of a flute. '珠露零' is a metaphor for something precious and fleeting, while '箫韵清' evokes a serene and pure atmosphere.
  • 幽修凤语兮和且平 - Poetic description of the soft and harmonious sound of a phoenix's call. '幽修' suggests something subtle and refined, '凤语' refers to the mythical phoenix, and '和且平' means harmonious and peaceful.
  • 欢乐未极兮空复情 - Poetic expression of unfulfilled joy and lingering emotions. '欢乐未极' means joy that has not reached its peak, and '空复情' suggests that the emotions remain despite the lack of fulfillment.
  • 紫箫咽兮夜无哗 - Poetic description of a purple flute sounding mournfully in the silent night. '紫箫咽' evokes a somber and melancholic tone, and '夜无哗' emphasizes the quietness of the night.
  • 宝篆微袅兮烛垂花 - Poetic description of a fragrant incense stick (宝篆) burning slowly, with the candlelight casting shadows. '微袅' means to rise slowly and gently, and '烛垂花' describes the flickering candlelight.
  • 河欲没兮夜欲阑 - Poetic description of the river about to disappear (metaphorically) and the night drawing to a close. '河欲没' can be interpreted as the river fading away, and '夜欲阑' means the night is nearly over.
  • 聊逍遥兮暂为欢 - Poetic expression of taking a moment to enjoy oneself. '聊逍遥' means to casually enjoy oneself, and '暂为欢' suggests a temporary moment of happiness.
  • 脱花钿兮收明榼 - Poetic description of removing jewelry and collecting a clear container. '脱花钿' refers to taking off ornamental hairpins, and '收明榼' means to gather a clear vessel.
  • 舒衾裯兮归洞房 - Poetic description of spreading out bedding and returning to the inner chamber. '舒衾裯' means to lay out bedding, and '归洞房' refers to going back to the private bedroom.
  • 齐交颈兮如鸳鸯 - Poetic description of two people lying close together like a pair of mandarin ducks. '齐交颈' means to lie with heads together, and '如鸳鸯' refers to the mandarin duck, a symbol of love and fidelity in Chinese culture.
  • 银漏短兮欢娱长 - Poetic expression of the shortness of time (silver water clock) compared to the length of enjoyment. '银漏短' refers to the short duration indicated by the water clock, and '欢娱长' means the enjoyment lasts longer.
  • 但悲白日兮上扶桑 - Poetic expression of sadness as the sun rises over the mythical tree of the sun (扶桑). '但悲' means only to feel sad, and '上扶桑' refers to the sun rising over the mythical tree.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase introduces the second song or verse, and it must be read as a continuation of the previous section.
  • Similarly, this phrase introduces the third song or verse.
  • This line is a poetic couplet, with each half describing a different aspect of the scene. The first half focuses on the natural elements (dewdrops and flute), while the second half focuses on the mythical elements (phoenix and harmony).
  • This line expresses a sense of unfulfilled joy and lingering emotions, which is a common theme in classical Chinese poetry.
  • This line continues the poetic description, with the first half focusing on the sound of the flute and the second half on the visual imagery of the incense and candle.
  • This line describes the transition from night to dawn, with a sense of fleeting enjoyment.
  • This line describes the act of removing jewelry and gathering bedding, preparing for rest.
  • This line describes the intimate moment of lying close together, with a contrast between the shortness of time and the length of enjoyment.
  • This line expresses a sense of sadness as the sun rises, marking the end of the night.

  • 若吾师有以见处，即死无憾 - 若吾师有以见处 means 'if my teacher has something to advise,' 即死无憾 means 'I will have no regrets even if it costs my life.'
  • 此间僻在荒滨，人迹不到 - 僻在荒滨 means 'situated in a remote coastal area,' 人迹不到 means 'where human footsteps rarely reach.'
  • 茭葑之与邻，鸥鹭之与友 - 茭葑之与邻 means 'living beside reeds and water plants,' 鸥鹭之与友 means 'being friends with seagulls and herons.'
  • 娘子虽年芳貌美，奈命蹇时乖 - 娘子 is a respectful term for a married woman, 虽年芳貌美 means 'although young and beautiful,' 奈命蹇时乖 means 'but unfortunate in fate and timing.'
  • 盍若舍爱离痴，悟身为幻 - 盍若 means 'why not,' 舍爱离痴 means 'let go of attachment and delusion,' 悟身为幻 means 'realize that the body is illusory.'
  • 被缁削发，就此出家 - 被缁削发 means 'to wear the monastic robe and shave one's head,' 就此出家 means 'to enter monastic life.'
  • 禅榻佛灯，晨餐暮粥 - 禅榻佛灯 means 'the monk's bed and the Buddha's lamp,' 晨餐暮粥 means 'breakfast and evening porridge.'
  • 聊随缘以度岁月，岂不胜于为人宠妾，受今世之苦恼，而结来世之仇仇乎 - 聊随缘以度岁月 means 'to pass the days following the Dharma,' 岂不胜于为人宠妾 means 'wouldn't it be better than being a favored concubine,' 受今世之苦恼 means 'suffering in this life,' 而结来世之仇仇乎 means 'and making enemies in the next life.'
  • 遂落发于佛前，立法名慧圆 - 遂落发于佛前 means 'she shaved her head in front of the Buddha,' 立法名慧圆 means 'and took the Dharma name Hui Yuan.'
  • 王读书识字，写染俱通 - 王读书识字 means 'Wang read books and learned to write,' 写染俱通 means 'and was skilled in both calligraphy and painting.'
  • 不期月间，悉究内典 - 不期月间 means 'within a few months,' 悉究内典 means 'thoroughly studied Buddhist scriptures.'

  • 凡事之巨细，非王主张，莫敢辄自行者 - 凡事之巨细 means 'all matters, big or small,' 非王主张 means 'unless Wang approves,' 莫敢辄自行者 means 'nobody dares to act on their own.'
  • 而复宽和柔善，人皆爱之 - 而复宽和柔善 means 'and she was gentle, kind, and virtuous,' 人皆爱之 means 'everyone loved her.'
  • 每日于白衣大士前礼百余拜，密诉心曲，虽隆寒盛暑弗替 - 每日于白衣大士前礼百余拜 means 'every day she would pay over a hundred bows before the Bodhisattva Guanyin,' 密诉心曲 means 'secretly confide her innermost feelings,' 虽隆寒盛暑弗替 means 'even in the coldest winters and hottest summers, she never stopped.'

  • 拜住归，具白于母 - Classical phrase meaning 'Bai Zhu returned and reported to his mother in detail,' indicating a formal and thorough communication.
  • 得非窥墙儿乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is he not the boy who peered over the wall?' Used to identify or refer to Bai Zhu.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秋千 - Traditional Chinese swing, often associated with leisure and entertainment.
  • 《菩萨蛮》 - Classical Chinese poetic form, often used in lyrical and narrative poetry.
  • 南词 - Type of traditional Chinese opera or song, often associated with the Southern regions of China.

Proper Nouns

  • 拜住 - Proper name, likely referring to a historical or literary figure.
  • 宣徽 - Title, likely referring to a high-ranking official or noble.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 红绳画板柔荑指 - Poetic expression describing a woman's delicate fingers, implying her beauty or grace.
  • 东风燕子双双起 - Metaphorical expression using the image of swallows flying in pairs, suggesting a scene of springtime or romance.
  • 夸俊与争高 - Means 'boasting about beauty and competing in height,' likely referring to a playful or competitive interaction.
  • 更将裙系牢 - Means 'tie the skirt tighter,' possibly implying a desire to maintain modesty or control.
  • 牙床和困睡 - Metaphorical expression that could mean 'sleeping soundly on a bed,' possibly suggesting a comfortable or intimate setting.
  • 一任金钗坠 - Means 'let the golden hairpin fall,' which could imply a carefree or relaxed attitude.
  • 推枕起来迟 - Means 'push aside the pillow and wake up late,' suggesting laziness or indulgence.
  • 纱窗月上时 - Means 'when the moon rises through the gauze window,' setting a romantic or serene scene.
  • 门阑多喜气，女婿近乘龙 - Poetic expression meaning 'it can be said that 'the door is full of joy, and the son-in-law is nearly riding a dragon,' which is a metaphor for a highly favorable marriage.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宣徽 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial court, specifically the Xuanwei (宣徽使), who was responsible for various administrative and ceremonial duties.
  • 满江红 - A type of poem that is often used to express strong emotions, such as love, patriotism, or sorrow.
  • 拜住 - The name of the person who wrote the poem, likely a scholar or poet.
  • 速哥失里 - The name of the daughter of the third wife, who is being betrothed to Bai Zhu.
  • 门阑多喜气，女婿近乘龙 - A traditional Chinese saying that celebrates a good match, where '乘龙' (riding a dragon) is a metaphor for a highly favorable marriage.

Proper Nouns

  • 宣徽 - The title of a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 满江红 - The title of a type of poem.
  • 拜住 - The name of the person who wrote the poem.
  • 速哥失里 - The name of the daughter of the third wife.
